attributes.description: Alerts when the p99 write latency exceeds 50 ms (`cassandra.client.request.write.latency.99p > 50000 us`). Sustained high write p99 usually points to disk / commit-log pressure.
attributes.name: [Cassandra OTel] High write latency (p99)

investigation_guide.blob:
## Cassandra high write latency (p99)

### What fired
`cassandra.client.request.write.latency.99p` exceeded 50 ms on at least one node during the evaluation window.

### Why it matters
Writes in Cassandra go to the commit log and memtable; under healthy conditions they are very fast. High p99 writes indicate commit-log disk pressure, flush backlog, or GC pauses. Write amplification from large unbounded collections is another common culprit.

### Triage
1. Overview dashboard: confirm affected nodes and write volume.
2. Check disk I/O on commit-log volume.
3. Inspect memtable flush activity and pending compactions.
4. Look at GC pauses — writes are latency-sensitive to stop-the-world events.

### Remediation
- Use dedicated commit-log disks / faster storage.
- Throttle bulk-write workloads.
- Review schema for unbounded collections or wide rows.

### Tuning
- 50 ms is a generic threshold. Writes are typically <10 ms on healthy clusters; tighten if that matches your baseline.

esqlQuery.esql:
TS metrics-cassandra.otel-default
// p99 write latency in microseconds
| WHERE `cassandra.client.request.write.latency.99p` IS NOT NULL
| STATS p99_us = MAX(MAX_OVER_TIME(`cassandra.client.request.write.latency.99p`)) BY server.address
// Threshold: 50000 us = 50 ms; writes typically lower than reads
| WHERE p99_us > 50000
| SORT p99_us DESC
